textPlain: The common answer to "why do you fork instead of building an extension" is "these are not possible with VS Code extensions". Is this true? Or is it easier to monetize when it is a fork?
I've been using for over 4 months and I think it's great - both Python and R.  Plenty of DS features that make VS Code feel more competent as data exploration tool.

Basically R studio/Spyder + Cursor built on the Code - OSS behind VSCode.Potentially very exciting. The early builds a year or two ago that I tried were too buggy to replace R Studio, but a tight integration of good data tools, a good IDE, and modern AI coding assistants is going to be very powerful if posit nails this.
Not exactly new, but I do think it has promise as a MATLAB replacement for Python. Spyder already does quite a good job at that though.
i wish they had a more exhaustive list of all the features they offer beyond vscode or cursor, otherwise it's hard to evaluate what exactly it brings to the table.
Being able to install Gemini Code Assist is a big plus over RStudio.
Behold, a free-as-in-beer, source-available program that brands itself as "free and open source".These guys' PR is trying too hard.
Meh. Another vscode reskin. And calling it IDE is a bit presumptuous.Dataspell is IDE.
I think PyCharm does a pretty good job as being a MATLAB-like IDE replacement for Python.
Yep, ill give it a try, but i dont see myself switching away from VSCode.
